intro_paragraph: In 2026, opera occupies a paradoxical place in the cultural landscape: financially battered yet creatively restless, with legacy houses tapping endowments and slashing seasons [1][3] even as new commissions, free streaming platforms, and TikTok-native audiences quietly expand the art form's reach [4][6][8]. The Met has pursued a controversial Saudi rescue deal while European subsidies retreat in Berlin and beyond [2][5], yet audience-research data shows opera is still pulling in newcomers, just on different terms than the 20th-century subscription model that built it [7][9].

facts.claim: The Metropolitan Opera scaled back its 2026-27 schedule to just 17 productions, its lowest total in a non-truncated season since the company moved to Lincoln Center in 1966.
The Met has withdrawn roughly $120 million from its endowment since the pandemic, with the endowment dropping to $232 million from $306 million in 2022.

The Met signed a memorandum with the Saudi Music Commission to become the winter resident company at Riyadh's Royal Diriyah Opera House, a deal reported to bring the company more than $100 million over five years.
Met box office income fell to about $70 million last year, down from $90 million a decade ago, even as ticket sales recovered to 72% capacity in the first half of the 2025-26 season.

Arts Council England cut Royal Opera House funding by 11.5%, from £25.2m in 2022/23 to £22.3m for the 2023-26 portfolio period - a real-terms reduction the ROH says threatens the wider 'opera ecology' and talent pipeline.

Berlin's 2025 budget proposed roughly 130 million euros in cultural cuts - a 12% reduction - putting institutions like the Komische Oper, named Company of the Year at the 2024 International Opera Awards, in immediate financial danger.
English National Opera lost its £12.6m annual core grant in 2022 and is being relocated north, with Manchester productions and a new Greater Manchester Youth Opera Company launching in 2025.
OPERA America's 2024 Annual Field Report logged 2.09 million in total attendance for the 2022-23 season - a 23% rise over the prior year - alongside a 14% increase in artists employed.

OPERA America's national study of 11,000 operagoers across 36 companies (2020-2024) found 50% of new attenders had their first opera experience through recordings, while 52% cite ticket cost as the main barrier to returning.
facts.source_name: OPERA America - Understanding Opera's New Audiences

facts.claim: The Met's average single-ticket buyer age dropped to 44 in 2024 from 50 the year before - notable because single-ticket buyers now make up 75% of sales as subscriptions collapsed from 45% in 2000 to 15%.

OperaVision, the EU-backed free streaming platform, has clocked over 25 million views across all platforms since March 2022, averaging 30,000 views per day across a 30-company, 17-country partnership.
The Met: Live in HD enters its 19th season in 2025-26, broadcasting to more than 2,200 cinemas in 70+ countries, with over 30 million tickets sold since the series launched in 2006.
facts.source_name: Metropolitan Opera Live in HD - Wikipedia

facts.claim: Jake Heggie's 'Dead Man Walking' opened the Met's 2023-24 season - billed as featuring 'more recent work than any other season in modern Met history' - and has now received over 400 performances across 75 international productions.
OPERA America awarded $135,000 in 2025 Commissioning Grants to support new operas by women composers at five companies, on top of $100,000 in Discovery Grants to eight additional women composers.

Pablo Larrain's 2024 Netflix biopic 'Maria,' starring Angelina Jolie as Maria Callas, became opera's most visible 2025 pop-culture moment, with Warner Classics releasing a soundtrack of nine Callas opera extracts.
